sources.notes: Statement and constraints: remove at most k elements so the remaining sequence is p-towering; total n is 2e5.
Official editorial confirms binary search on p, prefix/suffix maximum p-towering subsequences, the monotone selected-set property, and the segment-tree implementation.

reference_candidates.notes: Luogu solution candidate; not consulted for this review.

annotation.summary: Given an array, delete at most k elements and maximize p such that the remaining sequence has a center whose values are at least p minus their distance from the center.
annotation.constraints: Sum of n is 2e5, values are up to 1e9, so p needs logarithmic search and each feasibility check must be near O(n log n).
annotation.core_idea: Binary-search p. For a fixed p, compute the maximum p-towering subsequence by combining the best increasing prefix side and decreasing suffix side around a center with a_i >= p. The prefix side has a crucial monotone property: as the scan moves right, one maximum selected set can be maintained by only adding positions. After setting deficit d_i = p - a_i, a position becomes selectable when its current deficit is non-positive; selecting it decreases all earlier deficits by one and removes the position by setting it to infinity. A lazy segment tree maintains range add, point set, and rightmost non-positive search. Run the same routine on the reversed array for suffixes, then check whether n minus the best combined length is at most k.
annotation.complexity: O(n log n log max(a)) per test file total, with O(n) memory.
tricks: monotone selected set: only add positions while scanning
rightmost non-positive search with lazy segment tree

solution_variants.name: binary-search-monotone-frontier-segment-tree
solution_variants.summary: Binary-search the answer. For each p, use a lazy segment tree to greedily maintain the maximum increasing p-towering subsequence for every prefix, repeat on the reversed array for suffixes, and combine both sides at each feasible center.

tags.evidence: The maximum p is found by binary-searching p and checking whether a p-towering subsequence of length at least n-k exists.
The feasibility check needs prefix range-add updates, point removal by setting infinity, and searching the rightmost position whose minimum deficit is non-positive.
For fixed p, the maintained maximum prefix subsequence only grows by repeatedly taking newly available positions; no selected position is deleted.
The proof relies on the monotone inclusion S_i subset S_{i+1} for maximum increasing p-towering subsequences over prefixes.
